title: Ethiopian Misir Wot (Red Lentil Stew)

subs.name: Toor dal (split pigeon peas) for a slightly nuttier stew; cook time increases by 5 minutes
0.5 tsp ground ginger powder in equal measure if fresh ginger is unavailable

ingredients.name: red chilli powder (or berbere spice blend)
visual_checks: Reduce to 1 tbsp for medium heat
ingredients.notes: Reduce to 1 tbsp for medium heat

subs.name: Kashmiri chilli powder for vivid colour with moderate heat, or paprika for mild heat with good colour
Ghee or butter for a richer stew (will no longer be vegan), closer to the traditional niter kibbeh version

steps.text: In a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat with no oil, add the finely minced onion. Cook dry, stirring often, for 12–15 minutes until the onion is reduced, dry and deep caramel-brown — this is essential for the characteristic sweetness and depth of Ethiopian wot.

Add the oil and stir to combine with the caramelised onion. Add garlic and ginger; stir for 1 minute until fragrant. Add red chilli powder, cinnamon, cardamom, coriander powder and turmeric; stir for 1 minute on medium-low until the spices are absorbed into the oiled onion base and the colour turns deep brick-red.

Add the rinsed masoor dal and stir to coat in the spiced onion base. Pour in 550 ml water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer, cover and cook for 20 minutes until the lentils are fully cooked and most of them have dissolved into the sauce — the stew should be thick enough to hold a soft mound shape.

Uncover and stir. If the stew is too thin, simmer uncovered for 5 more minutes to reduce. Squeeze in optional lemon juice and taste for salt. The finished misir wot should be dense, deeply red and intensely spiced. Serve hot over rice or with flatbread for scooping.

substitution_notes: masoor-dal → Toor dal (split pigeon peas) for a slightly nuttier stew; cook time increases by 5 minutes; red-chilli-powder → Kashmiri chilli powder for vivid colour with moderate heat, or paprika for mild heat with good colour; oil → Ghee or butter for a richer stew (will no longer be vegan), closer to the traditional niter kibbeh version; ginger → 0.5 tsp ground ginger powder in equal measure if fresh ginger is unavailable
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Misir wot is essentially a Berbere-spiced masoor dal — if you can cook Indian red lentil curry, this is the same technique with a different spice blend. All ingredients are from a standard Indian pantry. The dry-caramelising of onions before adding oil is the one technique to master. Pressure cook for 2 whistles if short on time, then reduce uncovered on the stove.
